title: Physostigmine
abstract:
Physostigmine
Its Use in Acute Anticholinergic Syndrome With
Antidepressant and Antiparkinson Drugs
Robert P. Granacher, MD, Ross J. Baldessarini, MD
We reviewed the use of physostigmine in the diagnosis and man-
agement of acute toxic psychosis due to drugs with anticholinergic
properties. The syndrome of agitation and toxic confusional psy-
chosis associated with peripheral signs of cholinergic blockade is
produced by several plant toxins, antispasmodics, ophthalmic prepa-
rations, and certain proprietary sedatives, as well as antiparkinson
medications, antidepressants, and some antipsychotic drugs.
Physostigmine, uniquely among the available reversible anti-
cholinesterase agents, can pass the blood-brain barrier to exert cen-
tral as well as peripheral cholinomimetic actions to reverse this syn-
drome. Psychiatrists should make more use of this safe, specific,
rapid, and effective treatment for anticholinergic drug toxicity, and
should particularly be alert to reversible anticholinergic brain syn-
dromes associated with antidepressants and antiparkinson medica-
tions, and even with antipsychotic medications.
The
ability
of
certain
drugs
with anticholinergic
actions to produce serious untoward neuropsychiatrie
effects is well established.1
s These drugs (Table) can cause
a syndrome with both peripheral antimuscarinic manifes¬
tations and signs of central toxicity. Central toxic effects
include delirium, anxiety, hyperactivity, hallucinations,
disorientation, and seizures. Peripheral toxicity is marked
by tachycardia, mydriasis,
facial flushing,
decreased
sweating, hyperpyrexia, decreased bronchial, pharyngeal,
nasal, and salivary secretions, urinary retention, and de¬
creased bowel motility. In addition, the tricyclic anti¬
depressants in particular can produce serious abnormal¬
ities in cardiac conductiuu and rhythm.8~lL-
Attempts to reverse the peripheral tOxlc effects of drugs
with anticholinergic properties have been made with the
reversible anticholinesterase agents (Figure). All of the
currently clinically available agents of this type, with the
exception
of ^physostigmino—(eserine)
salicylate,
are
charged
molecules that
pass
the blon^-hr*1'"—barrier
poorly.12 Physostigmine salicvlate (Antilirium) uniquely
has both central and peripheral cholinomimetic actions. In
1958, Forrer and Miller described the ability of this agent
to reverse the central as well as the peripheral effects of
doses of atropine as high as 212 mg." Since then, a few
other reports utilizing this treatment in the central anti¬
cholinergic syndrome have appeared.814-20 However, the
incidence of poisoning from drugs with anticholinergic
properties continues to rise,7-8 and evidence of a remark¬
able lack of appreciation of the specific, rapid, and effec¬
tive treatment for this syndrome still appears in the medi¬
cal literature.2123 Psychiatrists frequently encounter toxic
reactions
to
tricyclic
antidepressants319'28
and
anti-
p^rkjpsnn drugs,18 and the polypharmaceutical approach
too often used in psychiatry increases the risk of such re¬
actions. Also, complex clinical presentations involving a
mixture of drugs that include atropine-like agents in
cases of drug ingestions are often seen among psychiatric
patients.24
We have seen a variety of central anticholinergic syn¬
dromes successfully treated with physostigmine. Several
cases representative of the types psychiatrists are likely
to encounter in a general practice are selected for presen¬
tation. Psychiatrists in particular should be aware of the
effectiveness of physostigmine in the HiaiiagemenL öTan-
ticnoiinergic synaromes due to tricyclic antidepressants
and antiparkinson diugü'in addition to the many other
drugs with anticholinergic properties capable of produc¬
ing this syndrome. It is especially important to emphasize
the usefulness of physostigmine in the mana.g-ement of
tricyclic antidepressant toxicity, since our search di^inacd
feWef that! 15 iJlHih  »»* '" ' "** Amem-nn medical litera¬
ture. Moreover^ our experience indicates that there is a
striking lag in knowledge and application of this therapy
in the medical community in general.
Accepted for publication Dec 14, 1974.
From the Psychiatric Research Laboratories, Massachusetts General
Hospital and the Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School, Bos-
ton.

 REPORT OF CASES
Case l.-A 32-year-old foundry worker ingested 25 to 30 25-mg
capsules of doxepin hydrochloride (Sinequan). These had been pre¬
scribed by his family physician and had been in his medicine chest
for six months. In a setting of acute marital discord, he took them
in an effort to "quiet his nerves." He had a previous history of
using marihuana, occasionally barbiturates, and methampheta-
mine, up to 10 to 18 gm/day, and had been arrested one year pre¬
viously for possession of controlled drugs. Since that time, he had
sought psychotropic medications from a number of physicians.
Approximately four hours after the ingestion he was seen in the
Acute Psychiatric Service of the Massachusetts General Hospital.
He had agitation
impairment of recent memory, incoherent
sneech. dvsarthria, ataxia, dry
mucous membranes, and foul
-bj£alhJ_Ilis__Dulee rate was 90 beats per minute; blood pressure,
142/88 mm Hg; and oral temperature, 37.4 C (99.3 F). His pupils
were 4 mm in diameter and reacted sluggishly to light. Neurolog¬
ical and general physical examination findings were otherwise un¬
remarkable.
Since his wife brought the partially filled medication bottle to
the hospital, his signs were believed to be clearly due to doxepin.
He was ¡riven p>iySr((¡ti[Tnnino aalinyfate. 2 mp; by intramuscular in-
jection. Thirty-five minutes later his dysarthria had cleared; agi¬
tation was absent; speech was coherent; and his recent memory
was intact. Pulse rate was 84 beats per minute; blood pressure,
135/85 mm Hg; and oral temperature, 36.9 C (98.4 F). He was ad¬
vised to remain on the overnight ward for further observation,
but his improvement was so dramatic that he demanded to be al¬
lowed to return home. Arrangements were made for additional
care by his family physician and he left after an hour, free of
signs of anticholinergic toxicity. Follow-up at three days disclosed
that no additional treatment had been needed.
Case 2.-This case represents the use of physostigmine to coun¬
teract the central toxicity of benztropine mesylate. The patient
was a 43-year-old woman seen on the neuromedicai service of the
Massachusetts General Hospital for evaluation of a severe move¬
ment disorder. It was described as an idiopathic spasmodic retro-
torticollis, involving much of the axial musculature, and develop¬
ing in thp pfinrifl -if  bipolar manic-depressive illness of 17 years'-
duration
Shp had been exposed to a number of neuroleptic-anti-
psychotic and thymoleptic agents as well as electric convulsive
therapy in the previous seven years, and had been treated with
lithium carbonate, 1,050 mg/day, for about one year following the
onset of the movement disorder. In the course of evaluation of
various pharmacologie responses of the movement disorder, it was
found that thp mnrnmnntn diwiinirihnrl nntippphly aftpr adminis-
^p^nn ? _ apomorphine hydrochloride; partially alter benzU'Oulne
mesylate and diphenhydramine hydrochloride, but not after phy¬
sostigmine; and worsened with haloperidol (Haldol). Decreasing or
increasing the dose of lithium carbonate had no effect. This spec¬
trum of responses suggested that the disorder was pharmacolog¬
ically analogous to Parkinson syndrome. She was treated initially
with amantadine hydrochloride (Symmetrel), 200 mg/day, and
lithium carbonate was continued at 1,050 mg/day.
Within several hours of the addition of benztropine, she com¬
plained of severe dry mouth, and later became dysarthric, grossly
confused, disoriented, and ataxic. There was a moderate tachycar¬
dia (90 to 100 beats per minute); pupils were dilated (6 to 7 mm),
and her skin was warm and dry, but not flushed. All medications
were stopped, but there was little improvement during the next 24
hours. When it was realized that 15 mg of benztropine mesylate
had been given, she was immediately given physostigmine salicy-
late, 1 mg intramuscularly. Within 30 minutes, the patient's con¬
fused mental status improved dramatically, but by 60 to 90 min¬
utes, she was again confused and dysarthric. The same dose of
physostigmine was repeated and her mental status again im-
proved, with only some mild lingering confusion. The pulse rate
and pupils remained unchanged. A final dose of physostigmine sa-
licylate, 0.5 mg, was administered six hours after the first dose.
The next morning, 17 hours after the original dose of physostig¬
mine, the pulse rate, pupil size, and mental status had all returned
to their preintoxication state. During the intoxication, the move¬
ment disorder was strikingly improved. Benztropine mesylate was
gradually increased to 3.5 mg/day over the next several days,
with moderate neurological benefit without a return of the central
anticholinergic toxicity.
Case 3.—The third case involved an anticholinergic syndrome
associated with multiple neuropsychiatrie medications in a 23-
year-old mentally retarded man. He had been followed up by a lo¬
cal mental health agency for a number of years for impulsive be¬
havior and psychosis. His recent medication regimen consisted of
haloperidol, 6 mg; thioridazine hydrochloride (Mellaril), 300 mg;
and benztropine mesylate, 6 mg/day. He was seen at the emer¬
gency ward of the Massachusetts General Hospital disoriented to
time and place, violently agitated, and having visual hallucina¬
tions. He had a dry mouth and a pulse rate of 120 beats per min¬
ute; pupils measured 7 mm in diameter and were unreactive to
light. Rectal temperature was 38.2 C (100.7 F). He had stopped
taking haloperidol 24 hours earlier due to confusion, but had con¬
tinued benztropine and thioridazine. During the three days prior
to admission, he had received a total of 14 mg of haloperidol, 1,000
mg of thioridazine, and 16 mg of benztropine mesylate.
He was given 1 mg of physostigmine salicylate by intramuscu¬
lar injection. Within 45 minutes, his pulse rate dropped to 108 and
his mental status improved slightly, with a noticeable decrease of
agitation. However, at 75 minutes following the first injection of
physostigmine, a worsening mental status required a second in¬
jection of physostigmine salicylate, 1 mg. At that ime, his pulse
rate decreased from 116 to 104 beats per minute and rectal tem¬
perature remained at 38.2 C. Two hours later, the same dose of
physostigmine was repeated with additional improvement of his
mental status. He was admitted to the Erich Lindemann Mental
Health Center for further treatment. He was given only diazepam
(Valium), 10 mg every three or four hours for mild agitation and
continued an uneventful recovery during the next 24 hours. He
was discharged after 3 days, fully restored to his prior status.
Case 4.-This patient had a very complicated management prob¬
lem involving several drug interactions. A 23-year-old man was
seen in the Massachusetts General Hospital emergency ward fol¬
lowing two days of confusion, disorientation, and the delusion that
he had a transistor radio in his head. He was a patient in the Bos¬
ton City Hospital methadone maintenance program. When exam¬
ined, he was found to be writhing in bed, delirious, and with im¬
paired recent memory. Blood pressure was 140/100 mm Hg and
pulse rate was 120 beats per minute; rectal temperature was 37.8
C (100.2 F). His pupils measured 3 mm in diameter and did not re¬
act to light; mucous membranes were dry. Laboratory studies in¬
cluded normal fasting blood glucose and blood urea nitrogen val¬
ues, and except for mild leukocytosis,
a blood cell count was
normal.
He had initially been believed to be in a state of psychotic ex¬
citement and was given haloperidol, 10 mg by intramuscular injec¬
tion, but remained unchanged for two or three hours. At that
time, his family brought in an empty bottle that had contained
twenty 50-mg tablets of amitriptyline hydrochloride (Elavil).
Since the empty bottle and his clinical status suggested anti¬
cholinergic toxicity due to amitriptyline, he was given physostig¬
mine salicylate, 1 mg intramuscularly. Within 45 minutes, the de¬
lirium had cleared. He complained of drug-craving and showed
pilorection and was given methadone hydrochloride (Dolophine),
35 mg orally. Two and one-half hours after the initial dose of phy¬
sostigmine he again became delirious, and responded to physostig¬
mine, 1 mg intramuscularly. However, he then developed a notice-

rapid, and effective treatment for anticholinergic drug toxicity, and
should particularly be alert to reversible anticholinergic brain syn-

tions, and even with antipsychotic medications.

Case 3.—The third case involved an anticholinergic syndrome
associated with multiple neuropsychiatrie medications in a 23-

tions. He had a dry mouth and a pulse rate of 120 beats per min¬

light. Rectal temperature was 38.2 C (100.7 F). He had stopped
taking haloperidol 24 hours earlier due to confusion, but had con¬
tinued benztropine and thioridazine. During the three days prior
to admission, he had received a total of 14 mg of haloperidol, 1,000
mg of thioridazine, and 16 mg of benztropine mesylate.
He was given 1 mg of physostigmine salicylate by intramuscu¬
lar injection. Within 45 minutes, his pulse rate dropped to 108 and
his mental status improved slightly, with a noticeable decrease of
agitation. However, at 75 minutes following the first injection of
physostigmine, a worsening mental status required a second in¬
jection of physostigmine salicylate, 1 mg. At that ime, his pulse
rate decreased from 116 to 104 beats per minute and rectal tem¬
perature remained at 38.2 C. Two hours later, the same dose of
physostigmine was repeated with additional improvement of his
mental status. He was admitted to the Erich Lindemann Mental
Health Center for further treatment. He was given only diazepam
(Valium), 10 mg every three or four hours for mild agitation and
continued an uneventful recovery during the next 24 hours. He
was discharged after 3 days, fully restored to his prior status.

The ability of atropine-like compounds to induce acute
toxic psychoses and a syndrome of peripheral and central
manifestations of muscarinic receptor-blockade is widely
known. These agents include plant toxins, antispasmodics,
certain proprietary sedatives,
parkinson medications (Table). It has more recently been
realized that important aspects of the acute toxic effects
of many drugs used in psychiatry, particularly the tri¬
cyclic antidepressants (Table), can best be interpreted as
manifestations of cholinergic blockade.17·19-25"27 Further¬
more, rrniinp/ the rmenothiazines, thioridazjnp       to
ho pnrtimrnrly ntrnnçly intiirhnlinnrp-jr·^8·29 In contrast to
most other phenothiazines, with acute overdose it appears
to produce toxic effects that include manifestations of cen¬
tral and cardiac anticholinergic actions.30·31 Since cloza-
pine, which resembles the tricyclic antidepressants in
cholinergic properties than thioridazine, and is similar in
anticholinergic potency to benztropine,2" it might also be
predicted to produce central anticholinergic toxicity. Also,
antiparkinson medications used alone or in combination
with antipsychotic drugs can produce anticholinergic tox¬
icity in psychiatric patients.18
Many anticholinesterase agents (Figure)
the peripheral manifestations of anticholinergic intoxica¬
tion.6·32 However, it is still not generally realized that only
physostigmine can also enter the brain to antagonize the
central toxicity of drugs with anticholinergic proper¬
ties.1314·17 Moreover, the usefulness of physostigmine to
manage toxicity associated with psychopharmaceuticals
has rarely been reported in the psychiatric literature,1618·20
and is still not generally appreciated even outside of psy¬
chiatry.2123 For example, in a recent survey, we found that
only six of 20 community hospitals in metropolitan Boston
stocked physostigmine in their pharmacies. On the other
hand, all major teaching hospitals of the four eastern
Massachusetts medical schools routinely provided this
medication. Evidently, there is a lag in the dissemination
of knowledge concerning this treatment modality into the
general medical community. It is also our impression that
the potential usefulness of physostigmine is not widely
appreciated by psychiatrists, although the potential im¬
portance of physostigmine is particularly great in psychi¬
atry, since many of the drugs we employ have substantial
central and peripheral anticholinergic effects.
Physostigmine was originally derived from the Ordeal
Bean of Calabar (Physostigma venenosum balfour, Leg-
uminosae), a West African legume used to test the inno-
Structures of reversible anticholinesterase agents.
cence of persons accused of crime by their survival of a po¬
tion of the beans. As early as 1863, Fraser used it to treat
a number of conditions, including delirium tremens.3
sostigmine is a reversible anticholinesterase agent and,
unlike other similar drugs such as neostigmine and pyri-
dostigmine (Figure), it lacks a charged quarternary amine
moiety and thus crosses the blood-brain barrier to exert
central as well as peripheral cholinomimetic actions.12 The
toxicity of physostigmine presents an excessive eholiaar-
gic-parasympathetic stimulation.
dyspnea, epiphora, miosis, hyperhidrosis, emesis, diarrhea,
abdominal colic, biliary colic, urinary frequency, and
bradycardia,1218 Atropine sulfate should be available in
case of cholinergic excess and 0.5 mg can be given to coun¬
teract each milligram of physostigmine salicylate admin¬
istered.8 Since atropine has central as well as peripheral
antimuscarinic actions, it would seem preferable to use in¬
jectable anticholinergic agents lacking central actions,
yucll as glycopyrrolate (Robinul) or methscopolamine bro-
mme (ramine.). For this purpose, parenterally adminis¬
tered glycopyrrolate has been given at doses one tenth to
one fifth those of physostigmine to counteract unwanted
diarrhea.34 Similarly, methscopolamine has been given in
doses of 0.75 or 1.0, intramuscularly, to prevent peripheral
side effects ofJiQ--mg of -pkysQgtigmine salicylate.'·' The

tes, gangrene, coronary artery disease, heart block, peptic
Drugs and Chemicals That May Produce

matic attack or heart block, but the most common acute
toxic responses are nausea and vomiting.
Physostigmine not only has therapeutic importance as
illustrated in the cases presented, but can also be of great
superimposed on an underlying psychosis. In confusing
cases, physostigmine's ability to reverse the central ef-
fects of drugs with anticholinergic properties can be used
as a rapid and simple clinical diagnostic test. In the last
few years it has been shown to be specific and effective for
reversal of scopolamine-delirium in obstetric patients,36
postoperative reactions to atropine or scopolamine,'7 and
delirium due to poisoning from over-the-counter drugs
containing scopolamine.8·16 One of these reports contained
1,727 cases of successful reversal of central toxicity with
no untoward effects. Physostigmine is also reported to be
useful in the management of toxic uffecLu of oiurdoiieS òf

All four of the present cases represent drug-induced
acute anticholinergic psychoses the general psychiatrist is
likely to encounter: namely, those associated with tricyclic
antidepressants, antiparkinson agents, and even pheno-
thiazines. However, as the Table illustrates, this syndrome
should be cojisidered in all t.nxir· psyphnses as more than

Cases 2, 3, and 4 are more complicated clinically, and il¬
lustrate a number of important aspects of the use of phy¬
sostigmine. For example, these cases point out that due to
the relatively short half-life of physostigmine (one to two
hours), more than one dose is likely to be needed. Relapses
are frequent, probably due to the longer half-life of tri¬
cyclic antidepressants, antiparkinson drugs, and bella¬
donna alkaloids, in general. Seizures are a risk with any

be given by intramuscular or slow intravenous injection
(rapid intravenous injections may produce seizures). If no
cholinergic signs appear and if no clinical change is noted
within 15 to 30 minutes, an additional 1 or 2 mg of physo¬
stigmine salicylate should be given. Our clinical experi¬
ence indicates that this treatment should be continued un¬
til the patient's clinical status improves or cholinergic tox¬
icity is seen; otherwise the physician may question his
diagnosis and use insufficient physostigmine salicylate to
counteract anticholinergic toxicity..Pulse, mental status,

change, usually within a few minutes. Changes in pupillary

pecially with the belladonna alkaloids, and mydriasis may

needed in intervals of 30 minutes to 2 hours. Currently,
these must be given by injection, as oral dosage forms of
physostigmine need to be compounded on prescription.

Physostigmine is not a panacea for all acute brain syn-
dromes.19 However, it has been clearly demonstrated to be
effective for the rapid reversal of central anticholinergic
psychoses due to a variety of agents. If the diagnosis of
anticholinergic intoxication is suspected and
mental and physical evaluation demonstrates signs of pe¬
ripheral anticholinergic activity with an acute toxic hrain
syndrome, then its use is indicated. A rapid positive re¬
sponse is virtually diagnostic.14

histaminicsl. should not he used'. Physostigmine is a pow¬
erful adjunct for the psychiatrist in the diagnosis and
management of central anticholinergic toxicity, especially
due to tricyclic antidepressants, antiparkinson medica¬
tions, and even phenothiazines. More physicians should
become knowledgeable in its clinical use.
